title: Don't Give Up!
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Don’t Give Up! - Pastor Tito Lyro - Hebrews 4_14-5_11.txt

outline.title: Don't Give Up: You Have a Great High Priest (Hebrews 4:14)
subpoints: Without a high priest who intercedes before God, we are separated from him forever -- the ultimate catastrophe
Jesus is a great high priest because his once-for-all offering does not need to be repeated
Jesus has passed through the heavens, offering his blood in the heavenly sanctuary before the Father
Jesus is the Son of God -- fully God and fully man -- the only one who can reconcile man to God
Response: hold fast your confession (Jesus is Lord) rather than abandoning it under pressure

outline.title: Don't Give Up: Christ Gets It (Hebrews 4:15)
subpoints: Our high priest sympathizes with our weaknesses -- he knows experimentally what it means to be human
Sympathy means understanding what it is like to live in the middle of someone else's circumstances with a desire to help
Jesus was tempted in all points as we are, yet without sin -- his sinlessness made his temptations more intense, not less
Because he never broke, he experienced the full power of every temptation -- like a bar bent all the way through
Because he knows what we go through, he can help -- he always provides a way of escape (1 Corinthians 10:13)

outline.title: Don't Give Up: God Is Listening (Hebrews 4:16)
subpoints: Because we have a great high priest interceding for us, we have assurance to come before God boldly in prayer
The throne of God is no longer a throne of judgment for believers but a throne of grace
We come boldly not out of arrogance but out of confidence -- Christ is there saying 'this one may enter'
At the throne of grace we receive mercy for past failure and seek grace for present and future need
The supply of grace is unrestricted: the only condition is willingness to receive it in a sense of its indispensability

outline.title: The Qualifications of a High Priest (Hebrews 5:1-11)
subpoints: Every human high priest was taken from among men and could have compassion because he too was subject to weakness
No man could take this honor to himself -- the high priest had to be called by God, as Aaron was
Christ did not glorify himself to become high priest -- he was appointed by the Father, confirmed in Psalm 2:7 and Psalm 110:4
In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered prayers and supplications with vehement cries and tears -- and was heard
Though the Son, he learned obedience through what he suffered and became the author of eternal salvation

outline.title: Application: Flee to Christ, Not Away from Him
subpoints: The hour of trial and crisis is not the time to go away from Christ -- it is the time to flee to him
The Roman Catholic system of indulgences is directly contrary to the passage: there is only one mediator
There is no other sacrifice -- if you turn your back on Jesus, Hebrews 10 says no sacrifice remains
Come to the throne of grace first, not last, when struggling -- and expect to find mercy and grace

summary:
Preaching from Hebrews 4:14-5:11, Pastor Tito Lyro expounds the book's central pastoral appeal -- "don't give up" -- and grounds it in the person and work of Christ as the believer's great high priest. The sermon opens by contrasting the passage with the Roman Catholic doctrine of indulgences, which in 2008 offered forgiveness for those who completed a pilgrimage and certain rituals. Scripture teaches that there is only one mediator between men and God, and that one mediator has already done everything necessary.

The first movement focuses on Hebrews 4:14: the believer has a great high priest who has passed through the heavens. Pastor Lyro explains that without a high priest, there is no access to God -- no greater catastrophe is possible. The Levitical high priest could only enter the Most Holy Place once a year, hoping God would accept his offering, and had first to offer for his own sins. Jesus is great because his sacrifice needed to be offered only once, it was accepted (proven by the resurrection), and he entered the heavenly sanctuary -- not a copy -- with his own blood. Therefore, hold fast your confession and do not let go.

The second movement unpacks Hebrews 4:15: Christ sympathizes with our weaknesses because he was tempted in all points as we are, yet without sin. Crucially, his sinlessness made his temptations more intense, not less. Like a metal bar that is bent all the way through rather than breaking halfway, Jesus bore the full force of every temptation. He knows exactly what the congregation is going through and can help -- 1 Corinthians 10:13 promises that God always provides a way of escape.

The third movement turns to the throne of grace in Hebrews 4:16. Because of Christ's intercession, believers can come to God boldly at any time for anything. The throne that was once a throne of judgment has become a throne of grace for those in Christ. There we receive mercy for the past and grace for the present and future. The sermon closes with an urgent appeal: flee to Christ, not away from him, in times of trial.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Hebrews 4:14-5:11

If you are able, would you remain standing for a moment longer? And turn to the book of Hebrews chapter 4. We will be reading from chapter 4 verse 14 to chapter 5 verse 11. As you know, the chapter breaks are not part of the original writing. This is a letter to be read continuously without the breaks. Chapter 4 verse 14, the letter to the Hebrews. This is the word of our Lord.

*Seeing then that we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus, the Son of God, let us hold fast our confession. For we do not have a high priest who cannot sympathize with our weaknesses, but was in all points tempted as we are, yet without sin. Let us therefore come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need. For every high priest taken from among men is appointed for men in things pertaining to God, that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins. He can have compassion on those who are ignorant and going astray, since he himself is also subject to weakness. Because of this he is required, as for the people, so also for himself, to offer sacrifices for sins. And no man takes this honor to himself, but he who is called by God, just as Aaron was. So also Christ did not glorify himself to become high priest, but it was he who said to him, 'You are my Son, today I have begotten you.' As he also says in another place, 'You are a priest forever according to the order of Melchizedek,' who, in the days of his flesh, when he had offered up prayers and supplications, with vehement cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death, and was heard because of his godly fear, though he was a Son, yet he learned obedience by the things which he suffered. And having been perfected, he became the author of eternal salvation to all who obey him, called by God as high priest according to the order of Melchizedek, of whom we have much to say, and hard to explain, since you have become dull of hearing.*

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together.

*Father, we pray that you would use this portion of your word to help us see Christ more clearly, and as we hear it, help us also to become doers of your word. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.*

Please be seated.

## Introduction: Indulgences and the One Mediator

According to the Roman Catholic Church, August of 2008 to July of 2009 was the year of St. Paul. Pope Benedict XVI decided to celebrate Paul's 2000th anniversary of birth by offering a special plenary indulgence. I notice -- maybe you see -- the irony of the issue. Paul, who was the man known for preaching free grace and condemned any church that would preach a different gospel, is honored by the church actually selling forgiveness.

The conditions of the indulgence were clearly stipulated. Absolution would be granted by the Pope -- a plenary indulgence, good for any sin -- to the soul that does penance, receives communion, makes a pilgrimage to the papal basilica of St. Paul in Rome, and devoutly recites the Our Father and the Creed, adding pious invocations in honor of the blessed Virgin Mary and St. Paul. And if they did all that correctly during the Paul year, they might receive time off of purgatory -- up to full exoneration. Not a guarantee -- even then you might get a couple of years off, or maybe all the years, but the church never says how long you will be in purgatory anyway.

How contrary to the passage we have before us here today. The Scriptures teach that there is only one mediator. There is only one priest between men and God, and that is the man Jesus Christ. It is through faith in him that sins are forgiven -- not through some ritual and a declaration by the Pope.

The message the Holy Spirit has for this Hebrew congregation -- which was thinking it would be better to abandon Christ and return to Judaism -- is simple. And it is a message for us today. The message is: **don't give up your faith. Persevere to the end.**

Giving up the faith may not be a common temptation for most of us in that extreme sense. But there are days when the struggles, the pressures of the world, the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eye, the pride of life, the love of the world, the different heat that life brings makes abandoning faith attractive. Always having to swim against the current. We live in a region where being a Christian is not valued -- it keeps us from advancing at work, keeps us from fellowship with our families. Society has decided we are the pariahs of this world. And the pressure is great to give up, to return to something else, to do something that might be easier.

So I think we can all relate to the message of Hebrews: don't give up the faith. And it is not an empty exhortation either. It is grounded in the work of Christ.

## I. Don't Give Up: You Have a High Priest (Hebrews 4:14)

The first thing the Spirit tells us in verses 14-16 of chapter 4: **don't give up -- you have a high priest.**

"Seeing then that we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus, the Son of God, let us hold fast our confession."

This may not excite us much because we perhaps do not understand what a high priest is. But if you were to read the Bible from the beginning -- particularly Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy -- you would understand the necessity of having somebody who can stand before God on your behalf. The Bible is clear that none of us on our own can stand before God. God is holy and cannot behold sin. We are sinners by nature. We do not become sinners because we sin -- we are sinners because we are conceived that way, inheriting the corruption, pollution, and guilt of Adam's sin.

Not having a high priest who intercedes for you is to be separated from God forever. As you read the Old Testament, you see that only one family could function as high priest -- the Levites -- and even within that family, only the clan of Aaron could serve as high priest before the Lord. And even then, the high priest could only go into the Most Holy Place once a year. He had first to offer sacrifice for his own sins because he was just like us, a sinful man. Only if God accepted that sacrifice would he then come back and offer a sacrifice for the people.

We have a high priest, and his name is Jesus Christ. It is no longer a human one that has to go every year, hoping God would accept his offering. His offering has been accepted -- once for all -- and we do not have to live in fear, wondering if God accepts us. If you believe in Jesus Christ, God has accepted you. You have been brought into the beloved. He looks at you and sees the face of Christ. That is what God has done.

Verse 14 tells us at least three characteristics of our high priest. First, he is called a **great** high priest -- greater than the Levitical line because he offered the sacrifice that needs to be offered only once and does not need to be done again. Second, he has **passed through the heavens** -- his blood was not offered in the earthly tabernacle but in the heavenly one, presented before the very presence of God himself. The resurrection confirms that God accepted it. Third, his name is **Jesus the Son of God** -- a man who is also God, the one who can reconcile man to God and God to men.

So the response: "let us hold fast our confession." Hold fast. Grip it. Grip the truth that Jesus Christ is Lord. Romans 10:9-10 says that if you confess with your mouth the Lord Jesus and believe in your heart that God has raised him from the dead, you will be saved. Hold on to that. Do not let it go.

## II. Don't Give Up: Christ Gets It (Hebrews 4:15)

"For we do not have a high priest who cannot sympathize with our weaknesses, but was in all points tempted as we are, yet without sin."

Do not think that because he passed through the heavens, Christ is far removed from us. Our high priest **sympathizes** with what we go through in life. He is not oblivious to suffering. He is not oblivious to what it means to be human, to have broken relationships, to experience physical and emotional pain, to be sinned against. He knows -- he gets it.

Sympathazing means to know experientially our weaknesses. Not the weakness of being a sinner, but the weakness of being human. Paul Tripp describes it as understanding what it is like to live in the middle of someone else's circumstances, coupled with a desire to do whatever is within your power to help him out. That is what your Savior does for you.

Christ was tempted in all points as we are. He knows how hard it is to resist the temptations and pressures brought upon you by the world and the devil. He knows the heat of life. And because he knows, he can help.

But you might ask: how is it helpful to us that he was tempted without sinning? After all, he was perfect and we are by nature prone to fall. Does the fact that Jesus was perfect make the temptation he faced any less real and powerful? I contend that the fact that he was perfect, the fact that he did not sin, made all the temptations he faced all the more powerful.

Paul Tripp uses the illustration of a strong man bending rebar. He would get a half-inch-thick rebar and bend it -- when it got to 90 degrees, maybe the bar would break. Then he would get a one-inch thick rebar and bend it all the way to the end. Which bar required more pressure? The one that he was able to bend all the way through -- because it did not break halfway. That was Jesus. Jesus experienced the full power of every temptation because he never broke. So the fact that he was sinless meant he experienced everything to the full degree.

And because of that, he can sympathize with you. He knows exactly how much you and I can take. And he always offers a way of escape. 1 Corinthians 10:13: "No temptation has overtaken you except such as is common to man; but God, who is faithful, will not allow you to be tempted beyond what you are able, but with the temptation will also make the way of escape, that you may be able to bear it."

## III. Don't Give Up: God Is Listening (Hebrews 4:16)

"Let us therefore come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need."

Because we have a great high priest who is interceding for us before the Father, we have every assurance to come before God in prayer. As 1 John 2:1-2 says: "If anyone sins, we have an Advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous. And he himself is the propitiation for our sins."

The hour of trial and crisis and doubt is not the time to go away from Christ. It is the time to flee to him. He is listening. He is waiting for us to come to him.

Notice how we are to come: **boldly**, or with confidence. This is not arrogance but confidence -- based on the fact that we know we are going to be received by God because Jesus Christ is standing there on our behalf, as 1 Timothy 2:5 says: "There is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus."

Remember the story of Esther? She could not come before the king unless the king invited her -- and to come uninvited was to risk death. That is not how it is with our God. Here we have a much greater King saying: "Come in -- because of Jesus Christ, you have access to me at any time, for anything."

We call him Father -- even Abba, Daddy. He promises to answer all our requests according to his blessed will. The verse says we "will find" grace to help in time of need -- not "might find."

Westcott, the famous Greek scholar of the 19th century, does a great job of differentiating between mercy and grace: "Man needs mercy for past failure and grace for present and future work." Mercy is extended to man in his weakness, for all the darkness that is in his heart. Grace is to be sought by man according to his necessity -- for the present and the future. Paul says in 2 Corinthians 12:9: "My grace is sufficient for you, for my strength is made perfect in weakness."

John Calvin says: "We are indeed fully persuaded that Christ of his own accord stretches forth his hand to us -- who of us would not come in perfect confidence?" The problem is that the church is not really persuaded that Jesus Christ is extending his hand to us. If we were, we would be in constant prayer before that throne.

We need to pray: "Lord, I believe -- help my unbelief. Help us to believe that you are a God who is ready to give us grace any time we are in need."

## IV. The Qualifications of a High Priest (Hebrews 5:1-11)

Every human high priest was "taken from among men" and appointed for men in things pertaining to God. He could have compassion on the ignorant and those going astray because he himself was subject to weakness. He was not exempt from the human condition.

And no man takes this honor to himself -- the high priest had to be called by God, just as Aaron was. So also Christ "did not glorify himself to become high priest, but it was he who said to him, 'You are my Son, today I have begotten you'" (Psalm 2:7). And as Psalm 110:4 says: "You are a priest forever according to the order of Melchizedek."

In the days of his flesh, when he had offered up prayers and supplications with vehement cries and tears to him who was able to save him from death -- he was heard because of his godly fear. Though he was a Son, yet he learned obedience by the things which he suffered. And having been perfected, he became the author of eternal salvation to all who obey him.

## Closing: Flee to Christ

Church of Jesus Christ, flee to Christ. He is your great high priest who has paid the price for your sins and has given you access to the courts of heaven and the throne of grace. Don't give him up. Hold fast to him who is the author of your salvation.

I understand the pressures of this world. I understand the sufferings of this world. I understand the temptations of this world -- the heat that life brings that can cause you to think you would be better without Christ. But that is not the case. Don't give him up. Go further into him. Cling to him as if your life depended on it -- because it does. Not only this life, but the life to come.

Let us pray together.

*Father, thank you for our great high priest, the Lord Jesus Christ, who stands before you interceding for us. Pray that we would come to him, to seek him, to receive mercy and seek grace before the throne -- a throne that through his life, death, and resurrection became a throne of grace rather than a throne of judgment toward us. We pray that if there is any here struggling with their faith, they would run to Christ and be strengthened. We pray all these things in Jesus' name. Amen.*
